utterances: Right.
Okay.
Yeah.
On many levels.
Oh, Robert.
MS.
CROSSLEY: Let me just say this, though.
I think that - everything that Robert said is true about, you know, cynicism and all that's going to come into play as people start looking very carefully and picking him apart.
But I think what's really interesting is his language of the last several days.
He has said he is not a boomer.
Now, if I'm am an African-American - and maybe I'm just, you know, paranoid - I interpret that as, you know, I'm post-civil rights generation too, which says something different.

I'm a different kind of guy, I'm a different kind of black person; I think I can cross some boundaries that other folks haven't been able to cross, and I also think I can bring together some groups that haven't done it before.
According to Gan Zhang, a professor at Guangzhou Institute of Geochemistry, that sediment is full of toxic substances.
And so even if he in the end decides not to run, I just think the language and how he positions himself while others are trying to run around and boost him is very, very interesting.
It's something we should pay attention to.
Well, let me ask you about this.
When you talk about his language around generations, no one has a really great set of language around the post-civil rights generations.
There is, you know, civil rights generation and then all of - most of the terms are hip-hop generation, generation X.
Are you saying that he needs a term to describe who he is?
He keeps implying that he's a new generation of leader, but does he need more language around that?
MS.
CROSSLEY: Well, I think he will probably get some more language about it.
What I'm implying, what I'm just saying flat out is that he's making it clear I am not of the Al Sharpton/Jesse Jackson for lack of a better, you know, example at this moment.
You know, they did what they did, but I'm a different kind of leader.
I'm going to the different direction; I'm appealing to different groups.
And I think that that sets him up for an interesting race if he decides to run.
And these are the - and I just think it's interesting that those are the messages that he's putting out.
And for him to reach out and take boomer and say, I am not a boomer, is really pretty powerful.
Marcelo, what do you think about that?
Well, I think that, like in the previous story, this is really fundamentally an issue in the symbolic realm.
I think that the senator senses a huge hunger for change.
There's clearly here an appetite for a new vision, a new voice, and he is thriving in that context.
Now there is also a Rorschach effect here, which is people see what they want to see now.
As the campaign unfolds, if the senator joins the race, things will define - will come to be defined in a much sharper angle.
And at that point, these symbolic issues that are now somewhat amorphous will become very, very focused.
And we will know exactly what the senator means when he invokes this post-boomer, post-civil rights imagery in his language, in his demeanor, in the ethos he's crafting around himself.
Farai?
Yeah.
If I were to add on to that.
You know, if I could coin a phrase, instead of thinking of this as generation X, we might want to start thinking of this as generation Malcolm, as in generation - as in Malcolm X.
In this way, what we saw in the last election, we saw Deval Patrick win up in Massachusetts.
We saw Harold Ford run a very, very strong race in Tennessee.
We saw Michael Steele run a strong race in Maryland.
And I think with Obama you are starting to see a real generational shift among black candidates, and part of it is definitely certainly more of a - kind of a mainstream, post-civil rights agenda.
But on top of that, something that Obama in particular brings is that Third World element.
He is, you know, he's the son of a Kenyan and a white mother as well.

And so you have it becomes a mixture of both sort of the business and educational opportunities that a whole new generation of black Americans have had, but also linking it specifically with the immigrant waves and viewpoints that have also now started to change the debate of race within America as well.
So I think that is also definitely a strong, powerful symbol that he brings in.
I guess that's latter Malcolm X, not so much the early Malcolm.
Latter Malcolm.
Well, yeah.
It's a combination of it.
It's a combination of both the former and the latter.
All right.
Well, one more quick topic.
Reparations.
And, you know, African-Americans have been fighting for reparations for years.
You know, companies that still exist today profited from slavery, including leaving long records of what they'd done to sell slaves or insure slaves.
A federal appeals court revived at least part of a reparations lawsuit last week, and the companies could be held liable if they lied about their ties to slavery to avoid negative publicity.
Speed lightning round.
Callie, is this a move forward?
Half a step forward.
That's what I would say.
Because the rest of the case, the ruling seems most negative.
So it's a half a step forward.
And Marcelo?
MS.
CROSSLEY: Trying to be speedy, sorry.
(Soundbite of laughter)
No, that was great.
Marcelo?
Well, the devil dwells in the details here.
So it will be a function of exactly how the lawsuits proceed in terms of again making transparent which companies lied about what, what were the profits.
So we're in for an education.
Stay tuned.
All right.
Whoever has that cell phone is hurting me, personally.
But that's okay.
We'll move along.
Robert?
It's not me.
I can definitely - I'm pleading not guilty on that one.

I think it's a sort of - a lot of really - a lot of background noise to -pardon the expression here - that I just don't really believe that the whole reparations movement as such is really an effective way or an effective use of the community's energies, frankly.
And I think that this is - I think the larger lawsuit will eventually fade away.
Callie, we have been so efficient you get another go.
What do you think about the longevity of reparations where you've got people like, you know, Ogletree at Harvard, Professor Charles Ogletree saying we can make this happen.
Will it happen?
Will there be reparations eventually, in some way or the other?
MS.

CROSSLEY: Well, part of the problem is a lot of the descendants are dying, some of the ones in the direct conflict, such as the one that Professor Ogletree is representing, those people out in Oklahoma.
And I think that really the reparations is going to turn on the direct connection.
Because in this lawsuit, we saw the guys say - the judge say -well, you know, it's been 150 years and we can't prove the direct connection.
So the more cases that can advance that have that direct connection I think keeps the conversation going even if Robert thinks it's background noise.
Well, you know, I would say that I actually did recommend Marvel years ago.
It was shortly after ""Spider-Man 1"" had come out and the stock was actually selling off.
Now this was a tremendous movie, it was a big surprise to a lot of people - you know, could a superhero movie like this perform so well?
But in the face of success, the stock was selling off.

The stock market is often irrational like that, so I don't think we should necessarily assume that the largest opening of any ""Spider-Man"" film, or possibly any film ever - which will happen this weekend and I think, Neal - I don't think we should assume that means Marvel opens up next Monday.
I do, however, encourage us to look at the future of Marvel.
I like that you have it in your portfolio.
It's one of my stocks as well.
Everybody has to make our own decisions out there, but Marvel is changing its business model to participate by making movies directly itself.
You know, ""Spider-Man"" and others have been made by movie houses, big production companies, and Marvel has been getting a check written to them.

But starting next year, starting with ""Iron Man,"" actually, with Robert Downey Jr. playing the lead role, all of a sudden Marvel will be participating 100 percent for or against its successes and its failures.
And so that's when it really gets interesting.
All right.
Let's see if we can get some listeners in on the conversation.
They may have some actual real money invested in stocks or they're thinking about it.
Let's go to Greg.
Greg is with us from New Hampshire.
Yeah, how you doing?
All right.
I'm 56 and I appreciate you taking my call.
Me and my wife have been pretty frugal.
Our bottom of our pyramid's getting bigger and bigger as we get older.
Our money's going to be going to our children.
But what my question is is - I'm looking around for an upstart, high-tech company that's doing research in alternative fuel.
Because I think that's going to explode.
And I think a newer company would probably return me more money in the long term than someone at, you know, another big oil company that is just doing it a little bit on the side.
I was wondering what you thought about that.
And it's a very fine question, and I'm doing the same thing that Greg is doing.
I'm looking around for other companies that are participating on what I think is probably a boom here coming for alternative energy, which can take so many forces as you know, Neal.
I mean, it's wind, it's sun, it's even water power.
I mean, there is just a lot of different answers out there.
One company that I recommended in the past that I find at least really interesting to look at is called Suntech Power.
It is China's maker of solar panels.
Just like a lot of other Chinese companies, they can make them really cheap, they're driving down the cost over time.

And the amount of sun that is captured and held by these panels thanks to superior technology, which we as a society do a pretty good job of growing over time, means that they're getting more effective as they get cheaper.
So that company today, we're not talking about a really small company now.
That company is worth $5.5 billion today.
And it's STP on the New York Stock Exchange.

But if you look at that company - and there are number of others in the industry, I kind of favor Solar just because it feels like once we do it well, it's just so replenishable - so I really favor Sun.
(Unintelligible) if I mention one thing, (unintelligible), is we got - my wife is a big garden fan.
So I bought her solar-powered lights versus electricity, and that saved us a lot of money.
All right.
I'm delighted to hear that, you know.
Anyway, thank you very much.
I appreciate it.
: Thanks, Greg.
Certainly.
